name: Sluagh
description: The Sluagh (pronounced 'sloo-ah') are restless spirits of the dead in Scottish and Irish folklore. They are often depicted as a host of vengeful spirits, the souls of the unforgiven dead, or those who lived wicked lives. These spirits are said to fly in swarms like flocks of birds, darkening the sky as they pass. Some accounts describe them as fallen angels, rejected by both heaven and hell, forever doomed to wander the earth. They are often associated with the west wind, traveling with it and entering homes through open windows or chimneys to carry off the souls of the dying. The Sluagh are generally considered malevolent and dangerous, bringing misfortune and death to those they encounter. Offerings and rituals were sometimes performed to appease them or protect against their influence. They are a chilling reminder of the consequences of a life unlived well and the fear of an afterlife filled with torment and unrest. The Sluagh represent the darker aspects of the Celtic spirit world, a constant threat to the living and a grim fate for the wicked dead. Their presence underscores the importance of piety, good deeds, and a proper burial to ensure a peaceful transition to the afterlife.
subtitle: The Host of the Unrestful Dead
appearance: The Sluagh are often described as shadowy figures, sometimes resembling birds or flocks of dark shapes in the sky. Some accounts depict them as emaciated humanoids with glowing eyes, while others describe them as indistinct, swirling masses of darkness. They are generally perceived as being repulsive and terrifying to behold.
habitat: The Sluagh are said to inhabit the skies, particularly during the night. They are often associated with desolate places, such as moorlands, bogs, and graveyards. They are also believed to frequent areas where death has occurred or where negative energy is concentrated.
behavior: The Sluagh are generally considered malevolent and predatory. They are said to hunt for souls, particularly those of the dying or the recently deceased. They may also attack the living, bringing disease, misfortune, and even death. They are drawn to places where there is fear, sorrow, or unrest. Their movements are often erratic and unpredictable, making them difficult to avoid or defend against.

keyMyths.title: The Sluagh and the Dying
keyMyths.description: It is believed that the Sluagh attempt to steal the souls of the dying as they depart from their bodies. Closing windows and guarding the room were common practices to prevent the Sluagh from entering and claiming the soul.
keyMyths.source: Folkloric accounts across Ireland and Scotland

keyMyths.title: Sluagh as Fallen Angels
keyMyths.description: Some tales portray the Sluagh as fallen angels, cast out of heaven but deemed too evil for hell. They are forever condemned to wander the earth, seeking to corrupt and torment the living.
keyMyths.source: Certain regional variations of Celtic folklore

sources.author: W.Y. Evans Wentz
sources.description: A comprehensive study of Celtic folklore and beliefs.
sources.text: A comprehensive study of Celtic folklore and beliefs.

sources.author: Patricia Monaghan

sources.description: A detailed reference guide to Celtic mythology.
